paragraphs.summary: In this supervisory statement (SS), the Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) sets out its expectations of firms in respect of application of the matching adjustment (MA). The MA allows firms to adjus
The scope of this SS includes:

This SS is relevant to all UK Solvency II firms and the Society of Lloyd’s and its managing agents (collectively called ‘firms’ in this SS), where they are applying for, or have, permission to use the

As part of meeting the applicable eligibility conditions as set out in regulation 4 of the IRPR regulations and [Chapter 2 of the Matching Adjustment Part](/pra-rules/matching-adjustment#de98d1d8c3f54
[Deleted]

The PRA expects firms to assess their use of the MA taking into account the assumptions underlying the MA, as set out in Chapter [1A](/guidance/supervisory-statements/ss07-18---solvency-ii-matching-ad

Permission for use of the MA is subject to the MA eligibility conditions, including conditions for the assets and matching liabilities to which the MA is applied. This chapter sets out the PRA’s expec

The MA eligibility conditions define the features that the asset portfolio, and in some cases the individual assets within it, must have. These features, together with the ability to identify, measure
The PRA will review each asset portfolio on a case-by-case basis as part of the MA permission process, taking into account the evidence provided by the firm in its application.

For the purposes of demonstrating satisfaction of the MA asset eligibility conditions, the PRA expects a firm to consider all the features of the assets against all of the relevant MA asset eligibilit
The PRA expects firms to have a robust screening process in place to identify those asset features that could affect MA eligibility.

Firms should review the relevant terms and conditions or prospectuses. Where reliance is being placed on third-party data providers, firms should perform validation checks, for example by comparing ag

In the case of pairings or groupings of assets, firms should consider carefully how any such arrangements satisfy all the relevant requirements, including whether the assets on a paired or grouped bas

For example, for the purposes of assessing the eligibility of assets paired with derivatives, this would include firms identifying any break clauses that allow the counterparty to change the cash flow

The PRA expects firms to consider carefully, and be able to justify, the method by which pairing or grouping arrangements have been reflected in the assessment of matching and the calculation of the M

The PRA considers that the requirement in regulation 4(9) of the IRPR regulations that ‘the cash flows of the assigned portfolio of assets must be fixed and not capable of being changed by the issuers

Certain categories of early redemption or termination rights would clearly not meet the eligibility criterion for fixed cash flows in regulation 4(9) of the IRPR regulations, for example rights of red
However, there are other categories of rights of redemption or termination that the PRA considers are less likely to undermine the need for predictability of cash flows that underlies the requirement

Further, the PRA expects firms to demonstrate that they have considered the extent of reinvestment or other risks posed by any such redemption or termination rights, and have considered whether and ho

The PRA expects that, as a minimum, firms would be able to provide a similar demonstration for any other asset where cash flows vary with the underwriting risks set out in the MA eligibility condition

The PRA does not consider that the rolling of the forwards on expiry, combined with the purchasing or selling of the underlying bonds (ie rebalancing), together produce fixed cash flows over the full

Relying on such assumptions is not consistent with the MA eligibility conditions for an MA portfolio of assets to have fixed cash flows. The relevant portfolio of assets may change only in limited cir

The PRA expects firms to consider carefully the prudence of any transactions or arrangements they enter into for the purposes of the MA, including their behaviour under stress, and whether the associa

The PRA expects firms to model a projection of the best estimate asset cash flows to assess the quality of matching and to calculate the MA. For assets with HP cash flows, the PRA notes that firms may

The PRA also notes that the cash flows used to calculate the BEL are determined using a probability-weighted methodology. For consistency, the PRA expects that such an approach should be the default m
In deciding on a methodology, the PRA considers that firms may want to draw a distinction between assets exposed to economic variability and assets exposed to ‘event’ (or non-economic) variability.
Examples of economic variability include:
Examples of non-economic variability include:

For assets exposed to economic variability, there may be sufficient relevant and credible data that shows how the payment profile is likely to vary under different economic conditions. For assets expo
Regardless of the approach taken, the PRA expects firms to:
The PRA considers that for smaller exposures to callable bonds, it may be reasonable for firms to adopt a ‘yield to worst’ methodology. However, as the exposure increases, the PRA considers that this

For assets with event-driven variability, uncertainty in event estimation may mean that it is not practical to derive a probability-weighted estimate of future asset cash flows. In these circumstances
If a deterministic approach is taken, the PRA expects firms to be able to set out and justify:

The PRA requires the same level of rigour over expert judgements in the asset projection as elsewhere in the Solvency II balance sheet ([Matching Adjustment 5.4(3)](/pra-rules/matching-adjustment#2711
The PRA requires firms to maximise the use of market data consistent with the economics of the asset (Matching Adjustment 5.4(2)). Th

Irrespective of the methodology, the PRA would expect that in most circumstances the cash flow profile would be consistent with that used for fair valuation of the assets under International Financial

For assets with HP cash flows, firms should quantitatively assess the extent of any cash flow mismatch that could arise from changes to the expected payment amounts and/or the timing of those payments

The PRA also expects firms to have in place a process by which trades made within an MA portfolio are reported regularly to senior management. The PRA expects to be able to review such information as
